findings.title: Thread-safety test makes a real network request and has no assertion on thread safety
findings.detail: The new test `test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED` issues 100 real HTTP requests to httpbin.org (an external service). This means: (1) it will fail in offline/CI environments without network access, (2) it is slow and non-deterministic, (3) failures from the external service will be misattributed as test failures. The PR description says this is about thread safety, but there are no unit/mock-based tests that verify the thread-local isolation without network I/O. Additionally, if ThreadPool is None (import fails), the test silently passes without actually testing anything — there's no skip marker or warning. The test should use mocking (e.g., unittest.mock) to simulate 401/200 responses and verify behavior in isolation.

findings.title: Test silently skips when ThreadPool is unavailable instead of using pytest.skip
findings.detail: When `ThreadPool is None` (line 430), the test body simply does nothing and passes. This masks the fact that the thread-safety feature is untested. The correct pattern is `pytest.importorskip('multiprocessing.pool')` or `pytest.mark.skipif(ThreadPool is None, reason='...')` so that the skip is explicit and visible in test reports. As written, the test always 'passes' even when it ran zero assertions.

findings.title: num_401_calls initialized to None instead of 1
findings.detail: In init_per_thread_state(), `self._thread_local.num_401_calls` is set to `None`. However, handle_401() at line 179 does `self._thread_local.num_401_calls < 2` and at line 181 does `self._thread_local.num_401_calls += 1`. If handle_401() is ever invoked for a thread whose per-thread state was initialized via init_per_thread_state() but where __call__() (which resets it to 1 on line 220) hasn't yet run, this will raise `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'int'` (Python 3) or produce wrong behavior (Python 2). In the normal call flow __call__ always runs first, so the None is overwritten at line 220, but it is a fragile and confusing design. The value should be initialized to 1 to match the intent and match what __call__ sets it to. Similarly, handle_redirect() at line 168 sets it back to 1, which is consistent. The initialization should be `self._thread_local.num_401_calls = 1` to be safe and coherent.

findings.title: init_per_thread_state 'init' flag prevents re-initialization across requests on the same thread
findings.detail: The `init` flag ensures per-thread state is only set once per thread. This means if the same thread reuses an `HTTPDigestAuth` instance for a second, unrelated request, state like `last_nonce`, `chal`, `nonce_count`, and `pos` will NOT be reset to their defaults — they'll retain the values from the first request's session. While `num_401_calls` and `pos` are overwritten in `__call__` (lines 220, 217) and `last_nonce`/`chal` are only used when `last_nonce` is set, the `nonce_count` could be stale. This is arguably acceptable for nonce reuse (same nonce → increment count), but the design is brittle. More critically, if init_per_thread_state is only called from __call__, and __call__ unconditionally sets num_401_calls=1 and pos, the `init` guard is only useful for `last_nonce`, `nonce_count`, and `chal`. This is fine in practice, but the comment 'initialized just once per-thread' is misleading since some fields are reset on every call anyway.

findings.title: Possible UnboundLocalError for hash_utf8 if algorithm is unrecognized
findings.detail: This is a pre-existing bug, not introduced by this PR, but worth noting: if `_algorithm` is neither 'MD5', 'MD5-SESS', nor 'SHA', `hash_utf8` is never assigned, and line 108 (`if hash_utf8 is None`) will raise `UnboundLocalError`. This was present before the patch.

findings.title: Thread-safety test only checks Authorization header format, not actual concurrency correctness
findings.detail: The assertion `assert '"auth"' in r.request.headers['Authorization']` only checks that the qop field is present in the header, not that authentication actually succeeded (r.status_code == 200) nor that the thread-local counter prevented infinite 401 loops. A correct thread-safety test should also assert `r.status_code == 200` and verify that each thread independently managed its own `num_401_calls` counter.

findings.title: handle_redirect accesses _thread_local without calling init_per_thread_state
findings.detail: handle_redirect() directly accesses `self._thread_local.num_401_calls` (line 168) without first ensuring thread-local state has been initialized via `init_per_thread_state()`. If handle_redirect is somehow invoked in a thread that has never called `__call__()` (e.g., in a custom hook pipeline), this will raise `AttributeError`. The same applies to handle_401(). While the normal flow always calls __call__ first, a defensive call to init_per_thread_state() at the top of both handlers would be safer.

findings.title: Missing space around assignment operator in test
